messages: Hey — I just switched jobs recently and would like your help getting my communications right.
I want to prepare two short pieces: a private note to my previous team and a public announcement to my professional contacts.
For both, I'm aiming for a warm, professional tone — appreciative and concise.
The private note should mention I'm available to help with handover and say thanks for the collaboration.
The public message should be suitable for a general audience; nothing company-specific or technical.
I'd also prefer to keep personal names minimal and avoid sharing compensation or negotiation details.
Make the wording simple enough that someone outside the industry will understand it.
Keep the pieces short — ideally a single short paragraph each so I can paste them into an email and a post.
I don't want them to read like a brag list; more a friendly sign-off and a positive look ahead.
If that's clear, I can ask you to draft them in a minute.
Packing up my desk felt oddly emotional — I found a scribbled to-do from years ago that made me laugh.
A coworker gave me a ridiculous oversized mug on my last day; I swear it's bigger than my head.
On the way out someone taped a goofy photo montage on my monitor — totally unexpected and sweet.
I gave my small desk plant to one of the newer hires; it felt strange parting with something so tiny.
My commute changed quite a bit: a longer ride but I get to walk through a small park now and that helps.
HR had one last form that needed an extra signature; it took longer than I thought and I nearly missed a meeting.
The new office sent onboarding swag — a giant hoodie and a notebook with a logo I can't quite pronounce.
My partner joked that I'm collecting company hoodies like merit badges; maybe they're right.
I spent an afternoon sorting old emails and deleting clutter; the archive is suddenly more organized than my desk.
I bumped into a former teammate in the elevator and we traded little tips about recurring meetings and calendar tricks.
I heard that at the new place they do board games on Fridays — not what I expected, but it sounds fun.
I've been keeping a running note of onboarding questions, but sometimes I overthink how to phrase something.
My cat walked across the keyboard during a last-week video call and somehow stole the show.
People sent me paper cards as well as messages — the handwritten ones felt oddly meaningful.
My neighbors kept asking how the new job is, which turned into a mini-practice session for explaining my role simply.
It's funny how the small perks stand out: one place had amazing coffee, the other has a tiny gym I didn't expect to care about.
A friend suggested a big celebratory dinner, but I ended up wanting something low-key and familiar.
I keep promising myself I'll update my résumé this weekend, then I get distracted by laundry and a podcast.
I can't decide whether to keep an old company t-shirt as a memento or turn it into a rag — sentimental indecision.
There are a few practical tasks still hanging — updating accounts, notifying a vendor, changing an address — the usual moving checklist.
Okay, I'm ready to move forward with the actual drafts now.
Could you write a short private note I can send to the people I left? Make it appreciative and include a brief offer to support the transition.
Then please draft a concise public post for my professional circles that announces the change without getting into internal specifics.
Both should be compact — short paragraphs that I can paste directly into an email and into a public post.
Aim for a warm, forward-looking tone rather than a long list of accomplishments.
Please avoid mentioning compensation or any inside jargon from either workplace.
Keep individual names generic — refer to teams or roles rather than naming people where possible.
I prefer the private note first, then the public one, and no need for links or attachments in the drafts.
Make the language clear so someone outside of our industry would understand what I'm saying.
Thanks — please provide both drafts now, starting with the internal note.
